w11.title: Cleaning & household maintenance — washing machines and dryers

tech.body: Students learn how washing machines and dryers work, consume energy/water, and the difference between vented and condenser dryers.

math.body: Students calculate the cost savings of using eco/wash programmes and compare dryer types.

env.body: Students understand how laundry habits affect carbon footprint and water scarcity.

green.body: Students evaluate trade-offs between convenience, cost, and environmental impact, and compare standard vs. eco settings and vented vs. condenser dryers.

w11.resources: Calculator, electricity tariff (e.g. €0.30/kWh), water tariff (e.g. €0.002/L), appliance datasheets.
w11.intro: The average European household does 3–5 wash loads per week. In this worksheet you will investigate how much energy and water a washing machine uses on different programmes, compare types of tumble dryer, and calculate what you could save by choosing eco settings.

theory.title: Theory
theory.intro1: A washing machine is one of the biggest water and electricity users in a home. Most of the energy goes into heating water — not spinning the drum. Modern washing machines include eco modes that use less water and lower temperatures, though they often take longer to run.
theory.intro2: Heating water to 40 °C or 60 °C uses far more energy than running the motor. An Eco programme at 20–30 °C uses up to 60 % less energy but runs longer. The trade-off: longer cycle time but lower cost and less fabric damage.
theory.dryersBody: Clothes dryers are even more energy-intensive. Understanding the difference between vented and condenser dryers — and when to avoid using a dryer at all — can save money and reduce emissions.

vented.label: Vented dryer:
vented.body: pulls in room air, heats it, blows humid air out through a hose (must be vented outside). High energy use (typical: 2.5–3.5 kWh per load).

cond.label: Condenser dryer:
cond.body: no vent needed. Moisture is collected in a tank. Slightly more efficient (2.0–3.0 kWh per load) but slower.

heatpump.label: Heat-pump dryer:
heatpump.body: most efficient (1.0–1.5 kWh per load) — uses a refrigerant cycle like a fridge. More expensive to buy but cheaper to run.

theory.water: Water use: washing machines typically use 40–90 litres per cycle, depending on drum size and programme. Eco programmes tend to use less water because they spin more slowly and re-use rinse water more efficiently.
theory.formulaNote: Simplified: ~0.05 kWh per litre per 10 °C temperature rise.

worked.title: Worked example
worked.setup: A cotton 60 °C programme uses 1.8 kWh and 60 L per wash. The eco 40 °C programme uses 0.9 kWh and 45 L. The household does 4 washes per week.

act1.intro: An average 4-person household washes 220 loads per year.
act1.q1: Calculate the annual electricity cost for each programme.
act1.q2: Calculate the annual water cost for each programme.
act1.q3: What is the total annual saving switching from Cotton 60 °C to Eco 40 °C?
act1.q4: How many kg of CO₂ are saved per year (use 0.27 kg CO₂/kWh)?
act1.q5: Find an EU energy label for a washing machine online (rating A to G). What is the difference in kWh/year between an A-rated and a D-rated machine?

act2.intro: A household dries 180 loads of laundry per year.
act2.q1: Calculate the annual running cost for each dryer type.
act2.q2: Calculate the 10-year total cost (purchase + running) for each.
act2.q3: Which dryer is cheapest overall after 10 years?
act2.q4: If the CO₂ emission factor is 0.27 kg CO₂/kWh: (a) annual CO₂ emissions for each dryer type at 180 cycles/year, (b) kg CO₂ saved by heat pump vs. vented, (c) how many trees absorb that amount (~22 kg CO₂/tree/year)?

peer.title: Peer-to-peer tip
peer.dykBody: A clothesline or drying rack uses 0 kWh.
peer.q: If the household air-dries 50 % of loads instead of using a vented dryer, how much money do they save per year?

refl.q1: Why do Eco programmes take longer? Is that a problem in practice?
refl.q2: When would a condenser dryer be preferred over a heat-pump dryer despite higher running costs? (Think about space, installation, upfront budget.)
refl.q3: If every household in a city of 100,000 switched to an eco washing programme (saving 93.6 kWh/year), how many kWh would be saved city-wide? Convert to MWh and GWh.

ee.title: Additional tasks — for electrical engineering students
ee.intro: These additional questions are aimed at students enrolled in electrical or electronic engineering programmes.
ee.q1: Draw a block diagram of a washing machine (heater, motor, pump, control board, sensors); indicate typical power ratings of each component.
ee.q2: What type of motor is used in modern washing machines (universal motor, BLDC)? What are the advantages of BLDC?
ee.q3: Are household washing machines connected to single-phase or three-phase power? Explain why.
ee.q4: What protection must a washing machine have (thermal, overcurrent, leak protection)?
ee.q5: Explain how an NTC thermistor regulates water temperature in a washing machine.
ee.q6: A washing machine motor has a power factor of 0.75. Calculate the apparent power (VA) for a 500 W motor. Why does this matter for the electricity grid?
ee.q7: Draw a block diagram of a dryer of the type you choose (heater, motor, pump, control board, sensors); indicate typical power ratings of each component.
